text: The surgeon achieves pneumoperitoneum using the Hassan technique. The right side of the abdomen shows a grossly dilated colon with limited space between it and the abdominal wall. Blunt dissection is used to release ischemic patches sealed by the abdominal wall. Adhesions from a previous midline laparotomy are dissected. The stricturing cancer is visible. To create more space, the future ileostomy site is fashioned. An Alexis bag is used to deliver the anterior cecal wall, and a Foley's catheter deflates the bowel of air and liquid feces. A green lid maintains pneumoperitoneum.

After deflating the bowel, the surgeon achieves adequate space between the bowel and the abdominal wall. The right ureter is identified, and medial dissection begins. The left ureter is also identified, and the inferior mesenteric artery is divided using haemlocks. Lateral mobilization is performed, followed by division of the mesorectum to expose the posterior wall of the upper rectum.

The surgeon uses an endostapler to divide the upper rectum. The resected bowel specimen is delivered through an incision in the left iliac fossa. The bowel is resected at the descending colon level. An elephant tube is inserted into the proximal bowel and connected to an underwater seal. Table lavage is performed with ten liters of warm normal saline, with inflow through a Foley's catheter and outflow through the elephant tube.

The midline scar is visible on the abdominal wall. Inside the surgical field, the outflow limb of the bowel is shown without tension. A large, thirty-three circular stapler is seen, but it is unable to reach the proximal rectum. An end-to-side anastomosis is performed on the mid rectum, followed by an air insufflation test that shows no leakage.
The appendices epiploica are clipped to the fallopian tube to reduce tension on the anastomosis. A covering loop ileostomy is performed, visible in the surgical field.
